author: @laurencerusson62
text: Hello Robert i just thought of a good idea you see i used to make stained glass tiffany lamps and for this you had to use a very specialised  200 watt soldering iron with an iron clad tip don"t be tempted to use a copper tip use the iron clad use 1/2 " tip you can order one from KANZA CRAFT IN YORKSHIRE i know for a fact it would completely stop up any holes without the need for any sealant as long as you clean the joints and preflux you will get a perfect result as long as you keep the iron on the joint also a good idea is to use a gas blow torch to heat it up and clamp together then apply your solder Tin mans solder making sure to dip your tip in flux please take no notice if the temperature would be a problem to the melting point of the solder but you will find it very usefull to do a host of other projects that you need to do that dont use high temperatures and makes airtight strong joints hope that this has been some use to you.

author: @mikael5914
text: Hi Robert. One thing you might consider if upgrading with a bubbling unit is to put it before the radiator because bubbling the gas through water may increase moisture content and you would need to condense that out as much as possible before combustion. Also you could (or should) fill up the bubbling unit with steel wool to break down the gass bubbles to smaller bubbles and keep them in the unit longer because otherwise they'll just bubble up much too fast and barely cool or be cleaned. The steel wool will also scrub much of the sulfides out of the gass which makes it easier to store if that is something you might be interested in. Obviously this bubbler could replace the particle filter completely but cleaning out or replacing the steel wool is more tedious, expensive and wastefull than just replacing some sawdust or straw. Finally I would recommend the overall piping in this system to be of larger diameter. This will deacrease the resistance through the system. You might find that you need quite a powerfull fan to just suck all of the syngas through the whole system. Otherwise I'm a big fan of your whole setup. I like the way your mind works.

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: Problem with a bubbler (as previously suggested by some) is that it will reduces the mass of fuel/air mixture entering the cylinder due to the intake stroke having to suck harder against the hydro-head of water. Two foot of water being equal to approx one psi, so even just six inches of head would add a another ¼ psi reduction in the cylinder on top of the frictional resistance through the carb and manifold. Volumetric fuel ratio wise, that would result in a much bigger drop in power than it seems. I would expect the top end performance to be poorer (compared to petrol) anyway, so a bubbler sounds like a bad idea for this reason.  Oily wood chips sounds much better.  Also, water would condense out some of the oleoresin terpenoids that survive their passage through the combustion region in the generator thus reducing the calorific value at start up. The only issue I have come across with high moister content in engines is with high compression engines like diesels. It can increase NOx emissions. Guess  the Chines knock off is a low compression, long stroke, suited to  2 star fuel. So no worries there and a bit of ‘cold’ steam would help efficiency. As for a beginner welding thin stuff with standard size nozzles or rods — forget it. I would braze it but GG sounds like a good alternative. Engineering is all about constructing assemblages of practical compromises anyway. [But a ‘beer barrel’ ? Sacrilege, pure sacrilege!] FEMA looks as though they have designed the simplest system that ‘works’. The only improvement that springs to mind is injecting steam or atomised water into the combustion zone which would increase the H2 and CO production due to disassociation of the H2O with the hot carbonaceous fuel. Some times referred to as producer or steam gas. This would increase the total calorific value of the gas going into the engine.

author: @mindaugasvaskevicius1818
text: I'm looking forward to see how it works. to be honest, I don't think the cooler has high enough cooling capacity(not enough surface area) to condense the water vapor. In an old handbook about downdraft gas generators there is a chart: input wet chips 32, water 0.5, 43.1 air, output: dry gass 66.3, char 0.9, tar: 0.09, water 7.4 all units kg/h. So looking at the chart I think condenser will work, but not effectively enough to condense a significant amount of water vapor. The gas comes above 700 degree, particle separation is done, thumbs up, the first cooler needs to cool down to below 300 degree C. Well that might work with your cooler. then tar separation, another cooler and then water separator, at least what the handbook says. I never forced my self to make one, so I can't criticize beyond what the handbook says. If I find your email I'll send you that handbook, I think it is somewhere on the internet : handbook of biomass downdraft gasifier engine systems 1988.

replies.author: @mindaugasvaskevicius1818

author: @googacct
text:
I am enjoying watching your build and has gotten me doing some research into what it would take for me to build one. 
Regarding bubbling  the gas through water to cool it. I would think you would want the gas to be as dry as possible. I would think additional moisture would cause a reduction in power and also promote corrosion in the engine. If anything, I would think you would want to pass the gas through some kind of dessicant or maybe molecular sieves to remove as much mosture as possible. 
For the cooling tubes,  could you wrap copper pipe around the tubes and use that to create hot water.

author: @ENDALLIMMUNITY
text: Rob: I enjoy watching your videos. Future member when income goes back up. I would like to suggest a few things that may help in your success. The high temp silicone is your best friend when you are not a great welder. Make an extra effort to seal the unit because leaks are your worst enemy. Preferably before you fire it up because the sealant sticks better on clean metal. Use a clear tubing to go to the engine so that you can detect any tar because it will gum up an engine. Set the engine close to the wood hopper or mount the whole thing on top of the engine, so that it can be mechanically connected to the barrel to vibrate the feed material. Make the lid so that it is spring-loaded. This is for safety just in case you do have a leak and it causes a puff back.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice suggestions mate - cheers

author: @RustyShakleford1
text:
Many studies showing canola oil or motor oil as a bubbler is very effective at cleaning and cooling the gas. 
Think how oil attracts soot and dust like a magnet. That’s what it does to the gas.

After that run it through a large homemade charcoal filter 5 gallons metal bucket. When the filter is full or clogged just heat it up on the barrel to ignite the soot and debris collected in the filter. Reusable as well as the oil in bubbler can be burnt in the gasifier when full or soot. 
All in the wartime documents describe filtration and cooling.

author: @glenchoitz9497
text: 30+ years ago I built a gasifier by using an air tight outdoor wood furnace I had also built...Once wood had reduced to mostly char I simply reversed the flow of the outgoing gases from the flue, pushing them back down thru the char by shutting down the flue damper and using a small blower in the door to create positive air pressure in the stove to start the process into a pipe located in the ash chamber and then to exit the stove. For filters I used 2 buckets filled with water. The first to separate the particulates from the gas and the second to cool the gas. It worked, to my amazement, as I had only read about them and their basic principles so was a very much 'by the seat of the pants' build. I ran a 5hp genset but was never satisfied with it's performance overall...High rpms needed by the genset were hit and miss and hard to regulate. Enjoying your much better constructed build and can't wait to see the results.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate and thanks for sharing your set up

author: @ShilohSedlak
text: A bubbler is a very good idea as a final cleaner.. But they need a spark arrestor after them which can be made out of a piece of threaded pipe jam packed full of stainless steel wool as tight as you can hammer it into the pipe.. Please use gloves handling the stainless steel wool as I have made these and tried to unwind the wool and found that it sliced my fingers to ribbons as it is unbelievably sharp😬 I have tried to only use the bubbler as the spark arrestor and it blew up.. It did stop the spark at it.. But it destroyed it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: a bubbler isn't normally used in a gasifier mate nor Is a flame arrestor but nice tips if you want to include them

author: @michaelclayton632
text: To avoid those leaky weld issues you could make your radiator from black pipe that you can buy at any home improvement store.  It is not too expensive  and with some Teflon tape should not leak. Here's another idea as well. Build a sheet metal box around  the radiator  with 2 vents, a blower on one and the other a furnace duct. That way you can recover the heat. I live  near Edmonton  Alberta  in Canada so the heat would be welcomed by my chickens in the barn.  A carbon monoxide detector  on the output would be a good idea.

replies.author: @michaelclayton632
replies.text: The CO detector would be on the output of the radiator box. In a normal hot air furnace there would be a heat exchanger to isolate the combustion gases from the heated air side. If there is a leak, the detector could save your life.

author: @werxeh8027
text: I've been waiting for this series to conclude before commenting, absolutely love this though. It's something that Britain did actually do during WW2 for some military vehicles due to the need to conserve petrol, and I've watched videos where people have made a lawnmower motor etc run on this. Incidentally when looking into this several years ago, I did chance upon a company which make 20kW generators using this, which come installed on a pallate, I cannot remember the name of them at the moment unfortunately. Not cheap, I think it was around about £13,000, but for a commercial venture with enough land to coppice for themselves, it's certainly a viable carbon neutral power source

replies.author: @sarchlalaith8836
replies.text: Victrix gasworks?

replies.author: @werxeh8027
replies.text: Desmond Bagley perhaps not for some on demand static energy requirements, but when it comes to transport, the high capacity batteries we have available at the moment aren't themselves wonderful for the environment (albeit not in greenhouse gases I don't think) and they're also limited in lifespan. There is also the potential for them to catch fire, which is oddly more likely than petrol car catching fire and potentially more dangerous due to how rapidly and violently lithium battery fires can spread before you've noticed them. I certainly don't think we've developed an ideal personal transport power source yet, but a wood gas engine wouldn't be a terrible idea, just takes a while to get going (which might make people consider whether they really need to use the car at all of course). Plus you get charcoal out of it. And the filtered out tar has uses.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree mate - and I think you are right 13,000 does sound a lot
